coredata.dc:title: Platinum nanoparticle-graphene quantum dot nanocage as a promising Schottky heterojunction electrocatalyst for electrochemical detection of vanillin in baby milk powder
coredata.dc:creator: Hui, Jia
Ruiyi, Li
Zhenzhong, Ding
Xiaoyan, Gao
Junshan, Xu
Jinsong, Shi
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Facile synthesis of platinum nanocrystal and its improvement in catalytic activity still face great challenges. The paper reports one strategy for synthesis of platinum nanoparticle-serine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot composite (Pt-Ser-B-GQD). The Ser-B-GQD was prepared by pyrolysis of serine, boric acid and citric acid at 180 °C for 3 h and then reacted with chloroplatinic acid to form platiunm nanocrystal. The resultant Pt-Ser-B-GQD offers a nanocage-like structure composing of tiny graphene sheets connecting many ultrasmall platinum nanocrystals with an average particle size of 8.2 ± 0.32 nm. The combination of Pt nanocrystal with Ser-B-GQD results in the formation of Schottky heterojunction at the interface. The unique structure achieves to an improved catalytic activity. Pt-Ser-B-GQD was further used as Schottky heterojunction electrocatalyst for the construction of electrochemical sensor for the detection of vanillin. Because of excellent electrochemical properties, the sensor based on Pt-Ser-B-GQD exhibits a high sensitivity for electrochemical detection of vanillin. Differential pulse voltammetric peak current will increase linearly with increasing vanillin concentration in the range of 1 × 10−9-1 × 10−4 M with the detection limit of 2.8 × 10−10 Μ (S/N = 3). The proposed analytical method provides advantage of sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility and was successfully applied in the detection of vanillin in baby milk powder.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Platinum-graphene composite
Synthesis
Electrocatalyst
Electrochemical method

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (2021M70277), The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2021YFA0910203) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21576115). We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

The authors acknowledge the financial support from The Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (2021M70277), The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2021YFA0910203) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115 ). We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme for synthesis of Ser-B-GQD and Pt- Ser-B-GQD.
SEM (A), TEM (B), HRTEM (C) and XRD pattern of the as-synthesized Pt-Ser-B-GQD.
CV (A) and DPV curves (B) of the sensor in the PBS of pH 7.4 in the presence (a) and the absence of 20 uM vanllin (b).

The DPV curves at the pH value of 5.0, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0 and 9.0 (A), the relationship curve between the Ep and pH value (B), the suggested oxidation mechanism (C), and the relationship curve between the Ip and pH value (D).

DPV curves (A) of the sensor in 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.03, 0.06, 0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 and 100 uM of the vanillin (from bottom to top), and the relationship curve (B) of DPV peak current (Ip) with vanillin concentration.
Sensitivity comparison of different analytical techniques for vanillin detection.

row.entry: Poly(glutamic acid) functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes/graphite composite
Molecularly imprinted poly(1-vinyl-3-octylimidazole hexafluoride phosphorus)multi-walled carbon nanotubes@ polydopamine-carboxyl-single-walled carbon nanotubes composite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results for electrochemical detection of vanillin in baby milk powder samples (n = 5).

row.entry: Vanillin found by the proposed method (μg g−1)

head.ce:title: Platinum nanoparticle-graphene quantum dot nanocage as a promising Schottky heterojunction electrocatalyst for electrochemical detection of vanillin in baby milk powder

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences and Health Engineering, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences and Health Engineering
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences and Health Engineering, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yangzhou Rixing Biotechnology Co., Ltd, Yangzhou 225600, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yangzhou Rixing Biotechnology Co., Ltd, Yangzhou 225600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Facile synthesis of platinum nanocrystal and its improvement in catalytic activity still face great challenges. The paper reports one strategy for synthesis of platinum nanoparticle-serine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot composite (Pt-Ser-B-GQD). The Ser-B-GQD was prepared by pyrolysis of serine, boric acid and citric acid at 180 °C for 3 h and then reacted with chloroplatinic acid to form platiunm nanocrystal. The resultant Pt-Ser-B-GQD offers a nanocage-like structure composing of tiny graphene sheets connecting many ultrasmall platinum nanocrystals with an average particle size of 8.2 ± 0.32 nm. The combination of Pt nanocrystal with Ser-B-GQD results in the formation of Schottky heterojunction at the interface. The unique structure achieves to an improved catalytic activity. Pt-Ser-B-GQD was further used as Schottky heterojunction electrocatalyst for the construction of electrochemical sensor for the detection of vanillin. Because of excellent electrochemical properties, the sensor based on Pt-Ser-B-GQD exhibits a high sensitivity for electrochemical detection of vanillin. Differential pulse voltammetric peak current will increase linearly with increasing vanillin concentration in the range of 1 × 10−9-1 × 10−4 M with the detection limit of 2.8 × 10−10 Μ (S/N = 3). The proposed analytical method provides advantage of sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility and was successfully applied in the detection of vanillin in baby milk powder.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Vanillin, 4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde, is one main ingredient in the natural vanilla derived from beans and pods of tropical vanilla orchids [1]. As an additive of milk flavor, vanillin was commonly used as flavor enhancer in various foods, including milk powder, ice cream, biscuit, custard, pudding, chocolate and drinks [2]. However, the most of vanillin on the market are produced by chemical synthesis. The synthetic vanillin may be harmful to human health to cause headaches and nausea [3]. The GB2760-2014 stipulates that no flavoring agent shall be added to the formula food for the infants aged 0–6 months. Development of a reliable analytical method for detection of vanillin in baby milk powder is of great significance.

Some analytical methods have been developed for the detection of vanillin such as spectrophotometry [4], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [5], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], gas chromatography (GC) [7], gas chromatography isotope-ratio mass spectrometry (GC-IRMS) [8], near-infrared hyperspectral imaging (NIRHI) [9] and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS)[10]. Among these methods, spectrophotometry is simple, rapid and inexpensive, but its sensitivity is much lower than that of HPLC and GC-MS. CE gives high sensitivity, selectivity and rapidity for detection of vanillin, but it often requires one complex sample pre-treatment. GC-IRMS and GC-MS are most commonly used methods for detection of vanillin in the laboratory. However, they require the use of expensive instruments, complex sample pre-treatment and skilled operators. The electrochemical sensor has received a more attention because of its high specificity, low cost, direct detection, good selectivity and low sensitivity [11].

To improve the electrochemical detection signal, many metal nanomaterials have been investigated as the electrocatalysts for construction of electrochemical sensors such as palladium [12], gold [13], silver [14], iron [15], copper [16] and platinum nanoparticles [17]. Platinum-based nanomaterial is the most commonly used sensing material for electrochemical sensors, because of its good catalytic activity, electronic conductivity, biocompatibility and chemical stability [18]. Great effort has been devoted to synthesis of platinum nanomaterials to improve the catalytic activity and durability [19]. Karaphun et al. reported one seed-mediated growth for synthesis of platinum nanoparticles [20]. The PtCI2 dissolved in ethylene glycol was mixed with graphene oxide and then heated at 125 °C. The formed platinum-graphene composite exhibits a high capacitance of 169.8F g−1 at the current densities of 0.25 A g−1, because of the occurrence of various oxygen functional groups in graphene and Pt+ ions as the contributing to produce pseudo-capacitance. Lzumi et al. reported one magnetron sputtering method for fabrication of platinum nanoparticles by using platinum-nanoparticle-monodispersed ionic liquid [21]. Because of the formation of conducting poly(diphenylamine) layer between platinum nanoparticles and carbon support, the introduction of polymerizable [DPA][HSO4] brings an improved specificc activity for electrochemical oxygen reduction. The formed Pt nanoparticle displays excellent dispersion and ultrasmall size. However, the method is complex and time-consuming and requires the use of some special equipments. Nilesh et al reported an electrochemical deposition method for synthesis of Pt-Se-FTO nanocomposite [22]. By electrochemical deposition, selenium nanoparticles and platinum nanoparticles were in situ grown on the surface of fluorine-doped tin oxide nanocrystals. The resultant Pt-Se-FTO has been applied in the construction of electrochemical sensor for H2O2. The prepared sensor displays wide linear range of 0.01–40 mM, high sensitivity of 7.3 µA cm−2 and good selectivity. Yuan et al. synthesized a platinum nanoparticles/graphene nanoplatelets/multi-walled carbon nanotubes/β-cyclodextrin composite by ultrasound-assisted assembly [23]. The resulting composite has been applied in electrochemical detection of folic acid. The sensor displays low detection limit of 0.48 μM and good stability. Engin et al synthesized Pt nanoparticles/graphene/Nafion nanocomposite and its use as sensing material for electrochemical detection of siloxane [24]. The nanocomposite was made via one-step chemical reduction of graphene oxide and H2PtCl6. Due to high catalytic activity, its detection limit reaches to 0.55 nM. Although the synthesis of platinum nanoparticles have made many progresses, facile synthesis of Pt nanocrystal and its improvement in catalytic activity still face great challenge up to now.

In the study, we reported one strategy for synthesis of platinum nanoparticle by using serine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (termed as Ser-B-GQD) as reducing agent, stabilizer and n-type semiconductor. The resulting Pt-Ser-B-GQD offers one nanocage-like structure, ultrasmall size of Pt nanoparticle and Schottky heterojunction at the interface. The unique structure realizes improved electrochemical properties. The electrochemical sensor based on Pt-Ser-B-GQD provides advantage of high sensitivity, selectivity and stability. It has been applied in electrochemical detection of vanillin in baby milk powder.

Citric acid, boric acid (H3BO3), serine (Ser), chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6), potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6) and chitosan were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. Vanillin was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) (Na2HPO4-NaH2PO4-KCl, 0.1 M, pH 7.0) and 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]4− solution was prepared in the laboratory. All solutions involved in the experiment were prepared using doubly distilled water. The chitosan solution was prepared by dissolving 5.0 g of chitosan in 100 ml of 1.0 % (v/v) acetic acid. All other reagents employed were of analytical reagent grade or with the highest quality and were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) purified from a Milli-Q purification system was used throughout experiment.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) analysis was carried out on HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. SEM sample was prepared by placing one drop of dilute ethanol dispersion of the composites onto copper plate attached to aluminum sample holder, and solvent was allowed to evaporate at room temperature. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was conducted on the JEOL JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope at 200 keV. The sample was prepared by dispensing small amount of dry powder in ethanol. Then, one drop of suspension was dropped on 300 mesh copper. The TEM grids were covered with thin amorphous carbon films. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured on X-ray D8 Advance Instrument operated at 40 kV and 20 mA and using Cu Kα radiation source with λ = 0.15406 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on PHI 5700 ESCA spectrometer with mono chromated Al KR radiation. The Infrared spectrum (IR) was measured using Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were taken by Multimode 8 Force Microscope (Bruker, Germany). UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were measured on Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) image was taken by Multimode 8 Force Microscope. UV–visible spectrum was recorded on TU-1901 spectrometer with DH-2000 deuterium and tungsten halogen light source in the absorbance mode (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., China).

Citric acid (0.2 mol), serine (0.1 mol) and H3BO3 (0.05 mol) were mixed and then heated at 180 °C for 3 h. The collected Ser-B-GQD crude product was dissolved in ultrapure water to form a 100 mg ml−1 Ser-B-GQD solution. It was treated by dialysis with dialysis bag (3500 Da) to remove unreacted raw materials from the system and finally freeze-drying to obtain solid Ser-B-GQD product.

The Ser-B-GQD of 50 mg was dissolved in 10 ml of ultrapure water and heated to 95 °C. Rapidly added 1.5 ml of H2PtCl6 aqueous solution (10 mM) under stirring. After adding, stayed warm for 10 min and cooled to room temperature. Followed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 5 min and washing with ultrapure water for 3 times. The collected Pt-Ser-B-GQDs was re-dispersed in 1 ml of double steamed water and stored at −4℃ before use.

Glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 1.0 mm in diameter) was polished successively with 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05 µm alumina powder, and sonicated in a 6.0 M of nitric acid/doubly distilled water and ethanol/doubly distilled water for 20 min. The GCE was subjected as working electrode to cyclic scanning in 0.5 M sulphuric acid solution in a potential range from −0.1 V to 1.0 V. When the cyclic voltammogram was almost unchanged, the electrode was taken out, cleaned with ultrapure water and finally dried in stream of nitrogen [25]. To prepare the modified GCE, the Pt-Ser-B-GQD aqueous solution of 1.0 mg ml−1 was prepared by dispersing solid Pt-Ser-B-GQD powder in 1.0 wt% chitosan aqueous solution containing 1.0 wt% acetic acid. After that, its 10 μL was dropped on the GCE surface and dried in air. Before use, the modified GCE was stored at 4℃ in refrigerator.

Baby milk powder was purchased from local supermarket. Milk powder of 1.0 g was transferred to 10 ml of beaker. After add 10 ml ethanol into the beaker, it was stirred for 5 min to dissolve the sample. Followed by ultrasonic treatment for 1 h and centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 20 min. To obtain sample solution, the collected supernatant was transferred into 25 ml of volumetric flask and then fixed to the volume with PBS.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out on the CHI660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai, China). One conventional three-electrode system was used for cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements, in which the Ag/AgCl (saturated KCI) electrode, platinum wire and bare or modified GCE were used as the reference electrode, counter electrode and working electrode. All electrochemical experiments were carried out under room temperature, and all experimental solution was degassed by N2 for 15 min at least. Then, N2 atmosphere was maintained during electrochemical measurements. The DPV parameters were set to a scan rate of 4 mV/s, 50 mV pulse amplitude, 20 ms pulse width and −0.2 V initial potential. The potential amplitude of ± 5 mV and frequency of 0.01–105 Hz were adopted in the EIS measurements.

The synthesis of Pt- Ser-B-GQD includes the preparation of Ser-B-GQD and Pt- Ser-B-GQD (shown in Fig. 1 ). Ser-B-GQD was prepared by one-step pyrolysis of citric acid, boric acid and serine at 180 °C for 3 h. Citric acid acts as the carbon source for forming graphene sheets. Unique structure and chemical properties make citric acid molecules can be easily changed into the graphene sheets composing of sp2-carbon atoms via their intermolecular condensation and subsequent carbonization [26]. Serine acts as the functional reagent to introduce special chemical groups at the edge of graphene sheets. Since one serine molecule contains one aliphatic hydroxyl group, its introduction increases the charge density of graphene sheets by strong electron-donating effect. This helps to improve the electron/energy transfer between graphene sheet and functional groups at the edge of graphene sheets [27]. Boric acid acts as the dopant to introduce boron atoms into graphene sheets. Unlike nitrogen and oxygen, boron is an electron-deficient element. The doped boron atom can accelerate the electron mobility in graphene sheets and results in an improved electrochemical property [28]. More importantly, the synergy of serine and borion may realize an improvement in electrochemical property.

The as-synthesized Ser-B-GQD was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and the results were shown in Fig. S1. The result of TEM analysis reveals that the Ser-B-GQD consists of quasi zero-dimensional dots. The size of graphene sheets is in the range from 2 to 8 nm with an average sheet size of 4.53 ± 0.21 nm. There is one d-spacing of 0.26 nm on the HRTEM image, which corresponds to the (002) crystal face of graphene. The result demonstrates the pyrolysis of mixed citric acid with serine and boric acid at a high temperature of 180 °C can form graphene sheets. The height of graphene sheets in Ser-B-GQD is between −300 to 900 pm, indicating that there are 2–4 layers of graphene oxide-like structure. There are several strong IR absorption peaks and bands on the FTIR spectrum. The IR absorption peak at 3433 cm−1 is caused by symmetric stretching vibration of NH and OH bonds. Owing to the formation of hydrogen bonds between –NH and –OH groups, the IR absorption peak become a wide and strong characteristic. The peak at 3182 cm−1 is due to the IR absorption of unsaturated -CH bonds. The peak at 1698 cm−1 is the IR absorption caused by stretching vibration of CO bond in –COOH group. Because Ser-B-GQD includes many –COOH groups, the peak is the strongest IR absorption peak on the FTIR spectrum. The absorption bands at 1408 cm−1 and 1158 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of CH and CO bonds. The above results of IR analysis that Ser-B-GQD contains –NH-, –OH and –COOH groups.

Pt-Ser-B-GQD was synthesized by the reaction of Ser-B-GQD with H2PtCl6. In the synthesis, Ser-B-GQD acts as the reducing agent, stabilizer and n-type semiconductor. Due to a strong reduction ability, Pt-Ser-B-GQD can reduce H2PtCl6 into Pt0 and finally produce Pt nanocrystals. The study shows that the above reduction reaction can complete within 10 min at the temperature of 95 °C. At the same time, the part of Ser-B-GQDs were firmly immobilized on the surface of Pt nanocrystals by Pt-O and Pt-N bonds. The immobilization enhances the structural stability and water-solubility of Pt nanoparticles. As Ser-B-GQD is one kind of polydentate ligand, the Ser-B-GQD on the surface of one Pt nanocrystal strongly combines with Pt2+ on the surface of another Pt nanocrystal by Pt-O or Pt-N bonds. The combination induces a rapid self-assembly process occurring between different Pt nanocrystals and finally results in the formation of three-dimensional architecture composing of graphene sheets and Pt nanocrystals. The three-dimensional architecture brings an improved conductivity and structural stability.

The as-synthesized Pt-Ser-B-GQD was characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS, IR spectrum and Motte-Schottky plots. Fig. 2 presents SEM, TEM, HRTEM and XRD pattern of the as-synthesized Pt-Ser-B-GQD. The results of SEM and TEM analysis show that Pt-Ser-B-GQD offers a nanocage-like architecture with the rich of porous structures. The formation of these pores is attributed to the self-assembly process occurring between different Pt nanocrystals. In the proccess, Ser-B-GQD acts as a polydentate ligand to form a large network structure by binding to Pt2+ coordination on the surface of different Pt nanoparticles. The nanocage-like architecture provides an ultrafast electron/electrolyte transfer and brings an improved electrochemical performance. The TEM image reveals that Pt nanoparticles in Pt-Ser-B-GQD have an ultrasmall particle size of 5–10 nm with an average particle size is 8.3 ± 0.17 nm. Ultrasmall Pt nanoparticles were well dispersed on graphene sheets. No obvious agglomeration of Pt nanoparticles can be observed from TEM image. The small size helps to increase the electrochemically active sites of Pt nanocrystals and results in an improved electrochemical property. On the HRTEM image there are two layer spacing of 0.227 nm and 0.20 nm, which correspond to the (111) plane of Pt nanocrystals and the (002) plane of graphene. On the XRD pattern there are seven diffraction peaks at 38.2°, 44.4°, 64.6°, 77.6°, 81.7°, 110.8° and 115.3°, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (311) and (420) faces of Pt nanocrystals. All XRD patterns are well matched with that of Pt nanocrystal (JCPDS 04–0802), thus the above result confirms that the reduction of H2PtCl6 with Ser-B-GQD can form the Pt nanocrystals. In addition, we also noted that the characteristic XRD diffraction peak at about 26° cannot be observed on the XRD pattern. This is because ultrasmall Pt nanoparticles were well inserted between tiny graphene sheets. This blocks the combination of graphene sheets and results in a significantly reduced crystallinity, indicating a very weak diffraction intensity.

Fig. S2 presents total, O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, Pt 4f and B 1 s XPS spectra of the as-synthesized Pt-Ser-B-GQD. The total XPS spectrum contains five peaks at 74, 190, 284, 400 and 532 eV, which correspond to the Pt 4f, B 1 s, C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s XPS spectra. The result demonstrates that Pt-Ser-B-GQD consists of platium (Pt), boron (B), carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) elements. As Pt element only come from Pt nanoparticles, and B, C, N and O elements only come from Ser-B-GQD, the above result also confirms that Pt-Ser-B-GQD includes Pt nanoparticle and Ser-B-GQD. The high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectrum can be divided into three peaks at the binding energy of 531.4, 532.4 and 532.88 eV. These peaks correspond to the O-CO, COC and CO bonds, respectively. The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectrum can be divided into three XPS peaks. The peaks at the binding energy of 399.4, 400.1 and 400.9 eV correspond to the graphitic N, amide N and pyridinic N, respectively. In addition, we also noted that the peak area of amide N XPS spectrum is much bigger than that of graphitic N and pyridinic N. This is because during the synthesis of Ser-B-GQD, a large number of amide bonds were formed by the condensation of carboxylic acid at the edge of graphene sheets and the amino groups in serine. Thus, amide N become main N specie in the Pt-Ser-B-GQD. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum can be divided into four peaks at 284.3, 284.9, 285.9 and 288.2 eV, corresponding to the CC, CC, CO, CO and O-CO bonds. The result shows the existence of –OH and –COOH. The high-resolution Pt 4f XPS spectrum can be divided into four peaks at 72.4, 73.2, 75.8 and 76.3 eV. The peaks at 72.4 and 75.8 eV can be assigned at the Pt 4f5/2 and Pt 4f7/2 of Pt0. The peaks at 73.2 and 76.3 eV can be assigned at the Pt 4f5/2 and Pt 4f7/2 of Pt2+. The above results demonstrate that Pt nanoparticles contain Pt0 and Pt2+ species. Further, Fig. S2E indicates the peak area of Pt0 is more than that of Pt2+. This demonstrates that Pt0 is main Pt specie in the Pt nanoparticles. The high-resolution B 1 s XPS spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 189.5, 190.0 and 190.8 eV. The peak at 189.5 eV corresponds to boron atom binding to carbon atom (B-C bond). The peak at 190.0 eV corresponds to the BC2O bond [29]. The B-C bond was formed by doping boron atom into sp2-carbon network of graphene sheets. The peak at 190.8 eV corresponds to the BCO2 [30].

Fig. S3 presents IR spectrum and Motte-Schottky plots of Pt-Ser-B-GQD. There are several IR absorption peaks and bands on the IR spectrum. The strong band at 3432 cm−1 is attributed to the IR absorption caused by the symmetric vibration of NH and OH bonds. The weak IR absorption peak at 2923 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of saturated CH bond. The peak at 1695 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CO bond. The peak at 1653 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CC skeleton in graphene sheets. The peaks at 1440, 1358 and 1137 cm−1 are caused by the in-of plane and out-of-plane bending vibration of OH. The above results of IR analysis demonstrates the existence of –OH, –COOH, –NH- groups in Pt-Ser-B-GQD. Fig. S3B shows the line section of two Mott-Schottky plots that give one negative slope. This demonstrates that both Ser-B-GQD and Pt-Ser-B-GQD belong to p-type semiconductors. Further, the Mott–Schottky plots are used for extrapolating the flat band potential (Vb) of Ser-B-GQD and Pt-Ser-B-GQD. The Vb value is − 0.18 V for Pt-Ser-B-GQD and − 0.78 V for Pt-Ser-B-GQD. These data show that a hybrid of Ser-B-GQD and Pt nanoparticle results in a more negative Vb shift, leading to an up-shift of the Fermi level of Ser-B-GQD. The up-shift of Femi level is beneficial to increase the electron concentration and electrical conductivity [31].

Classical chromogenic substrate, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), was employed as the redox probe for evaluating the catalytic activity of Pt-Ser-B-GQD. Fig. S4 indicates that in the absence of catalyzer no obvious color change can be observed from the reaction solution. This demonstrates that TMB is difficult to be oxidized in the absence of catalyzer. However, the colorless TMB solution will rapidly be changed into the blue product (ox-TMB) in the presence of Pt nanoparticles or Pt-Ser-B-GQD. This verifies that Pt nanoparticle and Pt-Ser-B-GQD can offer the high catalytic activity towards the oxidation of TMB. Fig. S4 also indicates the absorbance at 652 nm in the presence of Pt-Ser-B-GQD is much higher than that in the presence of Pt nanoparticles, indicating that Pt-Ser-B-GQD has a better catalytic acitivity compared with sole Pt nanoparticle. The enhanced catalytic activity is mainly attributed to the unique structure of Pt-Ser-B-GQD. In the structure, ultrasmall size of Pt nanoparticles makes the catalytically active sites can be fully exposed, nanocage-like architecture enables the rapid diffusion of dissolved oxygen to catalytic active center and improves the electron transfer efficiency of reaction system, and doped graphitic nitrogen changes the surface charge distribution uneven and brings an excellent adsorption and reduction ability of O2, and the Schottky heterojunction formed between Pt nanoparticle and Ser-B-GQD accelerate the charge transfer and improve the catalytic activity of Pt-Ser-B-GQD [32]. The above three factors improve the catalytic activity of Pt-Ser-B-GQD.

The CV and EIS behaviours of different electrodes were investigated in 1.0 mM K4Fe(CN)6 in the PBS of pH 7.4. Fig. S5 shows the bare GCE offers a lower CV peak current with a bigger charge transfer impedance (Rct). The modification of Pt nanoparticles brings an increase in the CV current and a reduce in the Rct value. This is attributed to high catalytic acitivity of Pt nanoparticles towards the redox of K4Fe(CN)6. The CV current will further increase if Pt nanoparticle was replaced by Pt-Ser-B-GQD. This is because Pt-Ser-B-GQD has a more electrochemically active sites. This speeds up the redox of K4Fe(CN)6 on the electrode surface and results in an increased CV current.

The CV curves of Pt-Ser-B-GQD electrode were measured in a 1.0 mM K4Fe(CN)6 in the PBS of pH 7.4 by varying the scan rates to investigate the kinetic behavior of electrode reaction. Fig. S6A shows the oxidation peak and reduction peak are symmetrical and have a small peak potential difference. This confirms that the electrode reaction is a quasi reversible redox process. With increasing the scan rate, the CV current will rapidly increase with a slight peak potential change. This demonstrates that Pt-Ser-B-GQD offers a high conductivity. Further, Fig. S6B shows the relationship curves between peak current and square root of scan rate. The peak currents of oxidation and reduction have a good linear relationship with the square root of the scanning rate, verifying that the electrode reaction is a process mainly controlled by diffusion.

To study on the response of proposed sensor towards vanillin, the CV and DPV curves of the sensor were measured in the absence and the presence of 20 uM vanllin. Fig. 3 A shows that in the absence of vanllin no any redox peak on the CV curve. In the presence of vanllin, the oxidation peak current increased obviously but the reduction peak current hardly changed. This is because vanllin was oxidized on the electrode surface and produce an increase in the oxidation peak current. Since this oxidation reaction is irreversible, there is no change in the reduction peak current. Further, Fig. 8B shows the DPV curves of in the absence and the presence of 20 uM vanllin. From Fig. 3B, we can see that the presence of vanillin causes a sensitive DPV response. As DPV detection has a better sensitivity and selectivity, it was selected for electrochemical detection of vanillin.

One vanillin molecule contains one hydroxyl group and its dissociation and protonation in aqueous solution may bring an obvious change in the electrochemical detection signal. Herein, the effect of medium acidity on the DPV peak current of the sensor for 20 uM vanllin was investigated. Fig. 4 A presents the DPV curves in the different pH values from 5 to 9. It can be seen from Fig. 4A that the DPV behaviour depends on pH value of the medium. This is because the degree of molecular dissociation in the different acidity exists in the difference of canillion species. The different vanillin specie gives a different oxidation performance. Therefore, in the different pH value the sensor exhibits a different DPV response. Further, Fig. 4B indicates the relationship of peak potential with the medium pH value. There is a good linear relationship between the peak potential (Ep) and the pH value. The corresponding linear regression equation is: Ep (V) = 0.0596 × pH + 0.2482, with Linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.983. The slope is close to the theoretical value of 0.0592, verifying the oxidation of vanillin is an equal electron and proton reaction process.

Based on the above results, one mechanism for the oxidation of vanillin was suggested at Fig. 4C [33]. Firstly, hydrolysis of vanillin produces 3,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde. This leads to the formation of methanol (CH3OH). Then, Pt-Ser-B-GQD catalyzes the oxidation of dihydroxybenzaldehyde and causes a sensitive electrochemical detection signal. With the oxidation of dihydroxybenzaldehyde, two protons and two electrons will leave the dihydroxybenzaldehyde.

Fig. 4D presents the relationship between the peak current (Ip) and the pH value. The Ip value reaches to the maximum when the pH value is about 7. To obtain high sensitivity, the PBS of pH 7.0 was employed for the electrochemical detection of vanillin.

Under the optimal conditions, the DPV curves in the presence of different vanillin concentration were shown in Fig. 5 . The DPV current will increase with the increase of vanillin concentration. This is because a higher concentration of vanillin results in a faster oxidation process of vanillin on the electrode surace. These oxidation produces an increased DPV current response. Further, Fig. 5B shows that the peak current (Ip) on the DPV cuve has a good linear relationship with the vanillin concentration in the range of 0.001–100 μM. The linear regression equation is: Ip (μA) = 0.0225 × C[vanillin, μA] + 0.0111, with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.998. The limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 0.00028 μM for the vanillin detection (N/S = 3). These results demonstrate that the proposed sensor gives a higher sensitivity compared with the other methods for detection of vanillin in Table 1 . Fifty duplicate measurement gives a 2.6 % of relative standard deviation, indicating a high reproducibility. The stability of sensor was examined by storing the sensor at 4 °C for three-weeks and then measure its DPV response towards 50 μM vanillin. The DPV peak current remain about 98.2 % after one three-week storage, indicating a high storage stability. Twenty-five sensors were constructed and used for electrochemical detection of 50 μM vanillin by the same procedure. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of DPV peak current responses among these sensors is 1.7 %. The same sensor was used the detection of 50 μM of vanillin twenty times. The RSD value of twenty times detections is 2.2 %, indicating a repeatability.

To evaluate the selectivity of as-proposed sensor, the influence of some inorganic ionic and organic compounds that often exist in baby milk powder on the DPV peak current response were studied. Fig. S7 shows that only vanillin (Van) can cuase on obvious DPV response. 1.0 mM of bovine serum albumin (BSA), glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), sucrose (Suc), oxalic acid (Oxa), caffeic acid (CA), Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Na+, K+, Fe3+ and Cu2+produce a change (Ip) of less than 5 %. The above results verify that the as-proposed sensor has a good selectivity for the electrochemical detection of vanillin. Thus, it can be directly applied in detection of vanillin in baby milk powder samples.

Baby milk powder samples were purchased from supermarkets in Wuxi city. All samples were pretreated by using the method described in 2.6 section. The obtained sample solution was used for detection of vanillin in baby milk powder by the proposed method and GC-MS. Moreover, the recovery experiment was also carried out by adding a certain amount of vanillin in the samples. Table 2 shows that the results obtained by using the proposed sensor are consistent with the results obtained by GC-MS [42]. Furthermore, the recoveries are in the range of 98.8 %–101.8 %, indicating good precision and accuracy.

The study demonstrated the design and synthesis of synthesis of serine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot-platinum nanoparticle hybrid. The as-synthesized hybrid offers three-dimensional structure, ultrasmall size of Pt nanoparticles and Schottky heterojunction. The unique structure greatly improves the catalytic activity and structural stability. The electrochemical sensor based on the hybrid provides advantage of sensitivity, selectivity and repeatability. It has been successfully applied in the determination of vanillin in baby milk powder. The hybrid also can be widely used in various fuel cells and industrial catalysis.

Jia Hui: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Li Ruiyi: Investigation. Ding Zhenzhong: Investigation. Gao Xiaoyan: Investigation. Xu Junshan: Investigation. Shi Jinsong: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (2021M70277), The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2021YFA0910203) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21576115). We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108320.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
